explanation_md: Brace elision distributes the 4 values across 2 `Point` objects: `pts[0] = {10, 20}` and `pts[1] = {30, 40}`. So `pts[0].x` is 10 and `pts[1].y` is 40.

prompt_md: You have `struct Point { int x {}; int y {}; };`. What does `std::array<Point, 3> pts { 1, 2, 3 };` produce for `pts[2].x`?

options.a: 3 — the third listed value goes to `pts[2].x`
options.d: Undefined behavior — reading an uninitialized field

explanation_md: With brace elision, values are distributed field-by-field: `pts[0].x=1`, `pts[0].y=2`, `pts[1].x=3`. Only 3 values are provided for 6 fields (3 Points × 2 fields each), so the remaining fields are zero-initialized. `pts[2].x` and `pts[2].y` are both 0. This is not a compile error — missing initializers are zero-filled.
The first color is pure red `{255, 0, 0}`, so `r+g+b = 255`. The second is pure green `{0, 255, 0}`, so `r+g+b = 255` again. Each sum is printed on its own line.

prompt_md: Which initialization syntax is safest and always works for a `std::array` of structs, including structs with user-provided constructors?

options.b: Explicit per-element syntax: `std::array<Point, 2> pts { Point{1,2}, Point{3,4} };`
options.c: Both are equivalent — brace elision always works for any struct
options.d: Neither — you must use assignment after declaration

explanation_md: Explicit per-element initialization always works for any aggregate or constructible type. Brace elision only works for aggregates (no user-provided constructors, base classes, or private members). When in doubt, use explicit syntax.
